text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'm going to explain Claude Code sub agents and agent teams. I'll cover what they are, how they're different, and then have them go head-to-head on a set of realworld tasks, including research, coding, and copywriting. So, if you're watching this video, you're probably familiar with Claude Code. It is one of many coding agents that we're seeing these days, which are essentially these AI agents that can build software. When you boil it down, claude code consists of two key components. The first is Claude, which is the underlying large language model that is taking requests and generating responses to those requests. But perhaps more importantly, the second key component of cloud code is all the tools and software that is built around the model. This includes things like giving it access to your local files, allowing it to do web search, allowing it to execute terminal commands, giving it the ability to compress past conversations, putting Claude into different modes such as thinking mode, a chat mode, and a mode where it'll keep generating text and performing actions until the task is done. It can also come up with to-do lists before doing complex tasks. and it can ask the user questions to gather more context about a given problem. So when we combine these two key components of this underlying large language model and a bunch of tools and features built around it, we get a coding agent like cloud code which is able to perform complex software engineering tasks and actually many other kinds of tasks as we'll see a little later. Although giving an LLM the right tools significantly increases the value you can get from it, relying on a single agent to perform complex tasks has its limitations. In other words, the bigger and the more complicated a given task is, the more context that it's going to require the LLM to process. And this presents a set of challenges. The first is that there are technical limits to how much text a LLM can process. This is defined by its so-called context window. For example, Claude Sonnet has a context window of 200,000 tokens, which is about the amount of text that you'll find in a long textbook. But within this context window, we have to fit all the information we want the agent to process. This includes things like a system message. So highle instructions for the agents purpose, the tools that the agent has access to, the user messages and the various requests that we're making to the LLM. If it's a thinking model, it's going to have these thinking or reasoning traces. That's all going to be in the context window. When the agent makes tool calls, that's going to be in the context window as well. Any result for the tool calls will be in the context window. And then the responses from the agent will also be in the context window. And on and on and on. However, if our context goes over this limit, then this information just won't be made available to the agent. However, even if all the context fits within the context window, there is another practical limitation that comes up when using these agents, which is the phenomenon known as context rot. This is the observation that the more tokens that are in the context window, this x-axis is number of tokens and then this y-axis is a performance on this specific task. As the context window becomes more and more full, so as we get to like 50%, 60% 70% of the context window, there's an observed degradation in model performance. So even if we're not hitting these technical limits of the context window, as the window gets more and more full, the agent can struggle to perform tasks well. This raises the importance of context management. So just making sure the right information is in the context window at the right time. And one very powerful way of doing context management is by using so-called sub aents. This is just the idea of delegating tasks to a fresh specialized instance of claude code. The way this works is that the user will send a request to claude code, but instead of claude just doing the task itself, it's able to delegate some if not all of a task to a sub agent. So what this looks like is we can call the instance of Claude that we're interacting with as a user the main agent and then the main agent will spawn this sub agent with a narrow purpose. For example, if we ask Claude code to research all the different coding agent tools that are available today, instead of it just going off and doing that itself, it can spawn a sub agent to help it do the research. And then what happens is the sub agent will do some work, produce some results, and then report the results back to the main agent who can then synthesize everything and present it to the user. But sub aents aren't just limited to one. Claude can spawn any number of sub agents that it likes. This is very helpful for research tasks. So the main agent can spin up multiple sub aents. They can all do research with different search terms or researching different domains. And each of them will produce results and they'll report these results back to the main agent who will then synthesize everything and respond to the user. Sub agents are really the combination of three key things. They are a model with tools and a purpose. So there are actually a few built-in sub aents that just come out of the box with cloud code. And if you've used cloud code at all, you've probably seen these spin up while it's working. The first sub agent is this explore agent which uses Anthropic's smallest model Haiku. It has access to a set of readonly tools and its purpose is to do file discovery, code search and basically understanding how a code base works. Another built-in one is a planning sub agent which uses the same model as the main agent. So if the main agent is sonnet, the planning sub aent will also be sonnet. If the main agent is opus 4.6, then the planning sub agent will also be opus 4.6. And the plan agent also only has access to readonly tools. And this is spun up when it's trying to research a codebase and trying to come up with a implementation plan. Finally, there's a general purpose agent which is basically just a copy of the main agent. So the model will be whatever the main agent is. It'll have access to all the tools that the main agent has access to. This is going to be great for like complex research tasks or multi-step operations and just like more complicated things. But of course, you're not limited to these three sub aents. You can create your own. So if you for example want to make a agent that specifically does code reviews in a specific way, you can spin one up. And so ultimately the way you create a sub agent is you create this text file. And the text file has two key parts. There's this like metadata part called the front matter. And this will have things like the sub agents name. It'll have a description of the sub agent that the main agent can review when it's trying to decide which sub agents might be helpful. It'll list out all the tools that sub agent has access to and it will define what model that sub aent will use. And then the second part are the instructions for the sub aent. This one is very short. It's just taken from the cloud code documentation. But you can imagine this can be a very long and sophisticated prompt for the sub agent. So especially if you have a specific way that you want code to be reviewed and audited and you have some coding standards that need to be met, this can all go in these instructions. The powerful thing about sub aents is that these are just typically invoked automatically. The main agent has access to the name and the description of all the sub aents that you've created and it can just decide which if any of the sub aents are relevant to the task at hand. Also, if you want the main agent to specifically use a sub agent, you can just tell it to do that. For example, if you've just implemented a new feature, you can specifically say use the code reviewer sub agent to review this code. Then there's no guesswork. Claude will call the sub agent that you want. However, there is still a problem with sub aents which is that all the information still needs to go through the sub aent. Here's again the picture of sub aents we saw a couple slides ago. And here is the bottleneck. So these sub aents can do a lot of work but only the information reported back to the main agent will be preserved. This brings up two key limitations. The first is that the sub agents can only communicate to the main agent. they can't communicate to one another. So for example, sub agent one and sub agent two are performing a task that has some kind of dependency on one another. Let's say agent A is building the front end, agent B is building the back end and it would be helpful if they could communicate with one another to make sure that the backend and the front end fit together nicely. However, with sub aents, they can't do that directly. They'll have to generate their output and then the main agent will be responsible for integrating these two things and if there are any issues the main agent will need to decide how to give the sub agents instructions on how to update the code whether that's updating the front end or the back end or whatever. And then the second limitation is that since everything has to go through the main agent we run into the same problem we saw earlier which is the context challenges. So the main agent has a limited context window and the main agent might be prone to context rot. So one way we can mitigate these limitations is through agent teams. And this basically just lets the sub agents talk to each other. Here we have that same picture. User makes a request to the main agent. But now instead of just spinning up a sub agent, the main agent will create this shared task list and spawn a full team of agents with different directives. And then these sub agents have the ability to interact directly with this shared task list. So they can claim tasks and they can mark tasks as finished without going through the main agent. They can just update this shared context directly. Another key thing is that the sub agents can talk to one another. So now if agent A is doing the front end, agent B is doing the back end and agent C is like a verifier, the front end and backend agents can freely talk to each other. Then the verifier can talk to both the front end and the backend sub aents giving them feedback, making sure that things look good. So each agent will just work independently doing their piece of the project and they'll just work till they're done. And then the main agent is kind of overseeing everything. So it'll keep an eye on the shared task list and then once everything's all said and done, it will review everything and make sure that the task was actually done and then it can interact with the user. As of recording this video, agent teams are still an experimental feature. So if you want to enable them, you have to do it manually. And so one way you can enable this is at the project level. So you can go into your project folder. You can create this.claude folder. So it's going to be a secret folder that Claude is going to look at. And then you can create this settings.json file. Then in that file you can just set this claude code experimental agent teams variable as one. So by default it's going to be zero meaning that agent teams are not enabled. And then you just change it to one enabling the agent teams feature. Then when you want claude to create a team you can just tell it create an agent team to do x y and z. You can also tell claude who you want in the team. So again, you can say make one agent focused on the front end, one focused on the back end, and one focused on verifying the code and running unit tests and things like that. We've talked about sub agents and agent teams, what they are, and how to set them up. Now, I want to review their similarities and key differences. The key similarity is that both these approaches allow Claude to delegate tasks to fresh Claude code instances. And this is a very powerful way of managing the context window of the main agent, especially things like research that can be very tokenheavy. And if Claude's going off and scraping web pages and doing a lot of different searches and a lot of different tool calls, a lot of this information is not going to be relevant to ultimately whatever task you're trying to do. So, it's going to just add a lot of noise to the context window and potentially harm performance. Some of the key differences, however, is that with sub aents, these sub aents can't talk to each other. With agent teams, these sub agents can talk to each other. With sub agents, we have this so-called centralized architecture. And so, in my previous video, I was talking about multi- aent systems where we saw these two key types of architectures. One was called centralized and one was called decentralized. The centralized architecture is where there's a main agent with a set of sub agents that can only interact with the main agent. they can't interact with one another. So there's a clear hierarchy between the orchestrator agent and the worker agents. On the other hand, the decentralized architecture was more of a swarm of agents where there is no hierarchy. All agents are equal to one another. They can all communicate with one another and they're just attacking the problem with no clear hierarchy. That would make agent teams this hybrid architecture where there is this clear hierarchy between the lead agent and the sub aents. But since the sub agents can talk to one another, that part of the architecture is decentralized. So agent teams mix the centralized and decentralized architectures I talked about in the previous video. Finally, the sub aent architecture is simpler and more fault tolerant. If a single sub agent makes a mistake, it's not going to blast it out to all the other agents propagating that mistake. Instead, it's only going to get passed to the main agent that will be able to review it and ensure that the output is correct before doing something with it. Agent teams on the other hand are more complex and there's this potential for error cascades because all these sub aents can talk to one another. It's possible that one of these sub aents makes a fatal mistake and spreads that misinformation to all the other sub aents leading to problems and a drop in performance. So the punch line here of when to use sub agents versus agent teams is sub agents are good for sequential tasks where you want this single stream of consciousness and you need to do task A then task B then task C. However, agent teams are really good for parallelizable tasks. For example, if the team is building a complex codebase, you can have one agent implementing the front end, another implementing the back end, or even the backend can be split into multiple pieces like the core logic and then authentication and then stripe integration. And you can have a sub agent responsible for each of these components and then they can talk to each other so they stay aligned. And this potentially allows claude code to build bigger, more sophisticated applications without the challenges of managing the context window effectively. The last thing I want to share is a mini experiment I ran comparing sub agents to agent teams. So what I did here is have these two approaches go head-to-head on three tasks. The first was putting together a lead list for local midsize businesses in the Dallas Fort Worth area. The second was building this app that would take a YouTube playlist and turn it into a distraction-free online course. And then the final task was designing and building a landing page. My hypothesis coming into it was that agent teams are going to be better for parallelizable tasks like doing the lead list creation while the sub aents will be better for sequential ones like building this YouTube course creator application. And then for the landing page creation, I wasn't really sure because it's kind of like a mix of both sequential and parallelizable task. And then some key points with the experimental setup. I used identical prompts to kickstart the sub agent and the agent team with the exception of the first line. For the agent team, I specifically told Claude to use an agent team and not to do the task on its own. But other than that, the prompts were identical. Another key thing is I only did one run per task per approach. Basically, I just gave the sub agent approach and the agent team approach one chance to do the task. And this was mainly to keep cost down because these multi- aent systems can run for a long time and burn up a lot of tokens. And then to evaluate the performance of each approach on each task, I looked at things like the total runtime, the token count, and the output quality, which we'll talk about. And if you're curious, all the code's on GitHub at this link here and linked in the description below. Task one was gathering a list of 50 contacts from local midsize tech companies and I'm near Dallas, Texas. So that's what local means for me. The final output for this task is just a CSV file with 50 contacts from various midsized companies that might be worth reaching out to for my AI education services. Here are the evaluation results. Looking at runtime, agent teams was a lot faster. And this is just because the team was able to spin up multiple agents that were running in parallel. So the agent team was able to do the task 8 minutes faster. The next thing is the estimated number of tokens generated to accomplish the task. Here the agent team burned about 30,000 more tokens to accomplish the task. And this is just natural because not only did it create more sub agents, but also the sub aents have to talk to one another, which also increases the token cost. But surprisingly, the token difference wasn't as big as I expected. I expected it to be more of a multiple. So maybe like 2 or 3x the sub aent approach as opposed to just being like a marginal percentage of the sub aent approach. The next thing I looked at are the total number of contacts. So indeed both systems produced a list of 50 contacts. But the catch was the agent team only provided emails for eight of these people while the sub aent approach had an email for every single person. This was kind of surprising. I was expecting the agent team to do this task better. But I think maybe it wasn't as persistent in ensuring the task was done well. I guess I got complacent and just moved forward with eight emails instead of the full 50. The second task was building this little web app that would take in a YouTube playlist and turn it into a distraction-free course. First, I'll show what the final results looked like and then we'll go through those same metrics one line at a time. On the left, we have the sub aent app and on the right we have the agent team. Probably the most striking thing is that the sub aent design just looks a lot better. like the colors are better. Also, the video titles are getting cut off in the sidebar, but they're actually getting wrapped in the sidebar for the sub agent design. The other thing that really stood out is that the sub agent added a progress bar while the agent team app did not have a progress bar. But beyond that, all the nuts and bolts were there. Like you could add notes, you could mark a lesson as complete. All the videos in this playlist were indeed there. and it provided a natural interface to navigate through all the videos. But again, looking at these key metrics, the agent team ran faster, so about 2 and a half minutes faster than the sub agent approach. Again, the sub agent approach didn't burn as many tokens, so it did about 99,000, while the agent team did 111,000. But overall, I again will give this one to the sub agent, which was as expected because when building a simple single page web app like this, having that coherence that you get with just a single agent is helpful because everything can just fit in the context window. It's helpful to have the full context of what you're trying to build, what you've built, and you're not trying to coordinate with other agents. So overall, my assessment was that the sub agent app had a better design and had the progress bar, which I liked, while the agent team had a worse design and no progress bar. But outside of this, everything else was pretty equal. Another thing that stood out is that for this task, because it was not very parallelizable, the agent team approach, Claude did indeed create sub agents, but each agent was blocked by the previous agent. So even though there was this team spun up, the tasks still ran one at a time. So basically created three agents, but then they just ran one at a time, which is why we didn't get any major timesaving here. Finally, I had each approach create a landing page. So this wasn't just building a website, but also designing the landing page well and writing compelling copy. So a big part of this was researching best practices for building a landing page. Also researching my background in past educational offers. We'll look at the final results side by side. Again, I feel like the sub agent approach had a better design just looking at it. So, we have this join weight list button persistently at the top, which I think is a good approach. We have a nice header there. I like the FAQ section. The agent team did not create an FAQ section. However, when actually reading the copy itself, I feel like the agent team approach did a lot better. It also did the side by side of like who this is for, who this is not for, which is good. And then the last thing is this weightless signup field needed to be integrated with my ConvertKit account. And that indeed did work for both implementations. So that was cool to see. The final numbers here, surprisingly, the sub agent ran faster. And this turned out to be because the agent team spent a lot of time researching not only me and my background, but also researching copywriting and landing page best practices. So I think that's why the final copy was a lot better for the agent team approach. Again, we have the sub agent approach producing a lot fewer tokens. But I would say as a final verdict, the sub aent landing page had a better design, but the agent team landing page had better copy. So here there wasn't really a clear winner. If I wanted to move forward with this, I would use a little bit of both. This experiment didn't go fully as I expected. The agent team's approach was not as powerful as I had anticipated or hoped. But the main takeaways are as follows. First, the agent teams generally did things faster than just having a single agent that could spin up sub agents do it. However, even though it took less time, the agent teams generated more tokens across all three tasks, but it wasn't as much as I expected. Again, I was expecting a multiple like 2 3 5x the token generation. Another observation was that the sub agents consistently produce better outputs than the teams. And I think this is ultimately because agent teams is still an experimental feature. So my sense is that the low output quality might be due to the immature scaffolding rather than limitations of the agent teams architecture, this hybrid architecture mixing the centralized and decentralized approaches. I suspect as more people try agent teams and Anthropic gets more feedback, they'll make it better. But at this point, I'll probably just stick to sub agents for actually getting stuff done. And then maybe I'll use agent teams to run more experiments and just explore what's possible. But as a final note, this isn't meant to be gospel, just meant to give you a little flavor of the behavior of each of these approaches.
